item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor nitpick: both sulfur dioxide and hydrogen sulfide are gasses, so there's no reason to use different measurement units for them. Other than that, the writing is really good and I got to feel for the character during the last addendum. Expungement is good too, even if I'm not a big fan of it (nice Gagarin reference too). Very enjoyable overall

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oooooooooh…

I get it now, thank you. Man, sometimes I'm sooper-dooper dense!

Now I know how those people who say "I don't get it" to articles I think are totally self-explanatory feel. I guess we all need a little help now and then!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get the impression that he's in his body. When he says 'body may return', he means that the whole module gets 'rewound' in time, including replacing the food and water and air he's consumed and returning his body to how it was when they launched him.

It was supposed to rewind his mind, too, but it doesn't - he can remember everything. Which is a problem on several levels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Correct me if I'm wrong, but the poor cosmonaut in the tin can floating over the planet is not in his body, right? Through some anomalous means, he has been separated from his body and it has prolonged his life? Maybe I misread that.

This is a very cool SCP. I like it a lot. The inherent mystery of the GRU is always appealing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm… You're certainly right about the guy never mentioning a file. Author should answer that ASAP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps the doctor mentioned the file because the scp mentioned the archive? Just guessing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes, due to what I believe is a poor English skill of mine, I do not understand the article fully yet just know that it is a well constructed piece. This SCP is one of them.

So, in effort to get closer to its meaning, I must propose a question about this specific part;

SCP-1778: Opening the hatch. Not the one to the archive, I have seen plenty of that one… the other hatch. The one that opens to space. I did not dare. Food and water and air may return, my own body may return, but I remember. They told me I would not remember, do you understand? It would be the same ██ minutes, every day, for three months, and then-

Dr. ██████████: Wait, please. What file?

this part I particularly don't get. Body may return, to SCP-1778? So he becomes human again? And what is this ██ minutes referring to, perhaps a duration of time SCP gets to stay in his body?

and more interestingly, SCP-1778 never mentioned the word 'file', yet the doctor asks "what file?". May have left a mistake there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: GRU-P skip. Love it. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, and thanks for the input. Adding. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not really comfortable with the father calling his son using a full name. Sounds too official. A shortened "pet" name would be better ("Tolya" in this case).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked it, Dr. Light. : )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Compelling premise, good writing, eerie, feels- I love it. I think you had a good amount of exposition- it's easy to over-obfuscate scips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. A cool concept, and good characterisation. Personally I had got it before the third addendum and thought it explained a little too obviously what was happening, but it works for the character and as others didn't get it until then I think you should keep it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least I had it figured out by the end, but it did take all the way until the final addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt supremely lost during the description of what the satellite actually does (the reversing orbit thing), and further confusion over the first two addenda. The third explained things, but I almost think it explained too much.

I must admit confusion has always been an issue with this skip. I've tried to rework it several times, and the closest I've come to expressing it right is its current wording. Nevertheless, thanks again. : )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt supremely lost during the description of what the satellite actually does (the reversing orbit thing), and further confusion over the first two addenda. The third explained things, but I almost think it explained too much. But then, given what happens to the guy, I couldn't not upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a few odd grammatical turns here and there, but overall this was very well done.

This might be due to my own difficulty with English (Spanish speaker here). While I would very much appreciate specific observations on the matter (that would also help me polish my English further), I'm just happy you enjoyed it. : )

Edit: Also, I must admit it'd be far worse if I hadn't got a lot of some help from other users.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Risky. The concept is complex, so much so that I feel it's going to take me a few more times reading this to grasp it fully. With that being the case, the piece relies much more heavily on its execution, and that is where this shines. I really like the buildup with the logs, and the Cold War-era atmosphere you achieved with this gives this that "dry-yet-menacing official document" feel that I like so much with the SCP format. It could be that my biases towards space-based things and Soviets are driving me here, but I really enjoyed this.

There are a few odd grammatical turns here and there, but overall this was very well done. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much, kind sir. : )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes two lost cosmonauts in our collection and I love them both. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, weizhong! : ) And sorry for the long wait. I wanted to rework some issues with isolated sentences and make the whole article a bit shorter (not much, though) and, well, I didn't dare to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So glad that this has finally been posted.

item.title: Re: Another GRU-P space skip. Wee.
item.content:encoded:
You know, if this article is finally successful here, it's going to be funny: first in Spanish, then translated to English and then translated back to Spanish ;)

Anyways, I'm really glad to see that you finally posted it here. I already told you what I thought with the Spanish version, and reading it again, I can see it has been improved. Good job, Reach.

item.title: Re: Another GRU-P space skip. Wee.
item.content:encoded:
Nope. I planned to write it in Spanish and publish it there, but I chose to write it in English. I have other skips in the SCP-ES series (026, 053, 021 and 067) but this is in English only.

Edit: (Also, thanks for your kind words. I'm a bit overexcited right now.)

item.title: Re: Another GRU-P space skip. Wee.
item.content:encoded: Is this a translation from SCP-ES?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I thought "oh god a short newb scp with log collapsibles, this is gonna suuuuck" but then was pleasantly surprised by a compelling story, with a pretty good central character. I liked it a lot. I don't really have much else to say, other than you did a good job.

item.title: Another GRU-P space skip. Wee.
item.content:encoded:
Credits!
Thanks to:

Djoric, Weizhong, SoullessSingularity, Fantem and AndarielHalo for their private messages with regards to the original draft, which I was too coward to put in the Draft forums for a while. Also, to Eskobar, who helped me with the almost finished draft. And to Vlank; her support to the original version (in Spanish) made this happen.

Gracias. : )

Edit: Oh, this was modified to fit Djoric's Random Character Generator Challenge. It isn't a tale, granted, but part of the characterization was sort of a consequence of the word "peculiar" and its meaning.
